world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Host character present on screen, question displayed, alphabet scroll interface visible at bottom of screen with letter selection cursor
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Host character reacts to player answers; player input actively scrolls through alphabet options; timer constraint visual indicator present

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Screen transitions to Fast Money gameplay mode, different visual layout emphasizing speed mechanic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Time pressure increases; player must rapidly input answers; host character behavior changes to reflect higher-stakes mode

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Screen displays modified set layout reflecting the show's Bullseye Round format; point bank visual indicator shown
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Special round rules enforced; $5,000 starting bank visual indicator; up-to-$15,000 bank growth indicator displayed

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Excuse text overlay displayed (e.g., 'tax considerations' or 'a birth in the family'); Fast Money round skipped visually
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game skips to retirement sequence; host does not acknowledge Fast Money availability

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visual indicator of win count accumulation; automatic retirement sequence triggered
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game forces retirement regardless of monetary status; champion status visually communicated before retirement screen

environmental_storytelling.example: NES version recreates the original Richard Dawson-era set appearance to establish the show's period and authenticity; later versions transition to Ray Combs-era set design to reflect show timeline evolution

Host lookalike character (Dawson-resembling on NES) serves as avatar of the actual TV show host; physical interaction like kissing female characters mirrors TV show behavior, grounding player in the show's world
Questions presented as coming from '100 people surveyed' — this text/visual framing establishes the game's core mechanic as matching public opinion rather than objective answers
Winnings counter and point accumulation displayed on-screen; $200 win threshold and $5,000 Fast Money target visually reinforce game progression and goal states

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Reacts visually and gesturally to player answers; kisses female player characters to recreate authentic show moment
Displayed on-screen acknowledging answers; behavior adjusted for Combs-era hosting style differentiation

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Host Character (Louie Anderson/Les Dennis FMV - 2000+ versions)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Full-motion video character provides authentic performance reactions; heightened visual fidelity increases immersion in actual show format
Scrolls left/right in response to controller input; indicates remaining time through visual urgency cues; misspelling tolerance indicated through acceptance feedback
Triggers excuse display rather than Fast Money sequence; communicates defeat to player through narrative justification (tax/family reasons)

notes: Family Feud NES lacks traditional danger/safety/faction systems as it is a trivia puzzle game based on a TV show format. World-state manifestation is entirely tied to game progression states (question round → Fast Money → retirement). Visual communication is primarily about authenticity (host likeness, set design) and clarity of game mechanics (alphabet interface, point/money counters). No political situations or faction conflict exists in the game design. The game's 'world state' is essentially the game show set and its hosting persona.
